title: Cerebral Palsy
oneLiner: A permanent, non-progressive motor disorder from fetal/infant brain injury; commonest childhood motor disability, requiring lifelong multidisciplinary care.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 2-year-old boy presents with delayed walking. Parents note he “always seemed stiff” and started rolling over late. On exam, his legs are adducted and extended with scissoring, he walks on tiptoes (toe-walking), and ankle clonus is present. His arms are less affected, and he has a mild right-sided hand preference (but is left-handed). He sat at 12 months and is not yet walking. Birth history: born at 30 weeks, had respiratory distress syndrome, and required ventilation. MRI shows periventricular leukomalacia.

**Dissect the Vignette:**

- **Stiffness / scissoring / toe-walking** → Spastic diplegia, bilateral lower limb predominant — classic for periventricular white matter injury (prematurity)
- **Ankle clonus** → Upper motor neuron (UMN) sign — corticospinal tract damage
- **Right-sided hand preference at 2 years (left-handed)** → Asymmetric lesion; however, in spastic diplegia arms are often spared; if unilateral, suspect hemiplegic CP (stroke)
- **Delayed motor milestones (sitting at 12 months, not walking at 2 years)** → Motor delay, but note that CP is *non-progressive* — milestones should still advance, albeit slowly
- **Prematurity + RDS** → Major risk factor for periventricular leukomalacia (PVL) — ischemic damage to watershed areas in preterm brain
- **MRI periventricular leukomalacia** → Radiographic confirmation: bilateral cystic or gliotic changes around lateral ventricles

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Dyskinetic (athetoid) CP:** Child with fluctuating tone, writhing movements, delayed motor milestones, but spasticity is minimal. Often due to kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy) — ask about neonatal jaundice. Exam: choreoathetosis, rigidity, but reflexes may be normal.
- **Ataxic CP:** Presents with wide-based gait, intention tremor, delayed gross motor skills. No spasticity. Usually due to cerebellar injury (e.g., prenatal hemorrhage). Misdiagnosed as progressive cerebellar ataxia — key distinction: non-progressive course.
- **Hemiplegic CP (unilateral):** Child at 1–2 years with hand preference (early), arm held in flexed-adducted posture, leg circumducts in gait. Can mimic stroke; consider neonatal arterial ischemic stroke. MRI shows focal infarct.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Cerebral palsy (CP) is a group of permanent disorders of movement and posture caused by non-progressive insults to the developing fetal or infant brain (before age ~2 years). The motor impairment is often accompanied by disturbances of sensation, cognition, communication, behaviour, and epilepsy. “Static encephalopathy” — the brain lesion does not worsen, but clinical manifestations may change with growth.

**Why This Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** CP affects 2–3 per 1000 live births — the most common motor disability in childhood.
- **Stakes:** It is a lifelong condition; many children have associated disabilities (epilepsy ~40%, intellectual disability ~50%, feeding difficulties ~30%) that drive morbidity more than the motor problem itself.
- **Common Pitfalls:** 
  - Mistaking CP for a progressive condition (e.g., leukodystrophy, metabolic disease) — leads to unnecessary tests or delayed diagnosis.
  - Attributing all symptoms to CP when new focal deficits appear — suspect stroke or infection.
  - Underestimating treatable comorbidities: spasticity can be managed with botulinum toxin, baclofen, or surgery; early intervention improves function.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The Story of How CP Develops:**

The brain injury occurs during a critical window of development (fetal, perinatal, or early postnatal). Different timing and location produce different CP subtypes:

- **Preterm brain (24–32 weeks):** The periventricular white matter is vulnerable to ischemia and infection (chorioamnionitis). The watershed zones around the lateral ventricles are poorly perfused. This causes **periventricular leukomalacia (PVL)** — necrosis of oligodendrocytes → impaired myelination. Clinically → spastic diplegia (legs affected more than arms because descending corticospinal fibers to legs run closest to ventricles).
- **Term brain (36–40 weeks):** Acute hypoxia-ischemia (e.g., placental abruption, cord prolapse) damages the deep grey nuclei (basal ganglia, thalamus) due to high metabolic activity. This produces **dyskinetic CP** (choreoathetosis, rigidity). Alternatively, middle cerebral artery stroke (often unilateral) → **hemiplegic CP**.
- **Postnatal (<2 years):** Meningitis, traumatic brain injury, or encephalitis can cause diffuse injury → spastic quadriparesis or mixed CP.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

- **Spasticity** → UMN lesion: loss of inhibition of gamma motor neurons → hyperexcitable stretch reflex. Exam: velocity-dependent resistance to passive movement, clasp-knife phenomenon, clonus, Babinski sign.
- **Scissoring gait / adductor spasm** → Overactivity of hip adductors (UMN damage spares no thigh adductors but inhibits flexors).
- **Toe-walking** → Spastic gastrocnemius-soleus complex → plantarflexion at ankle.
- **Choreoathetosis** → Basal ganglia damage (putamen): loss of direct pathway inhibition → involuntary jerky (choreic) and writhing (athetoid) movements.
- **Ataxia** → Cerebellar damage: impaired coordination, intention tremor, wide-based gait.
- **Feeding difficulties / drooling** → Pseudobulbar palsy from bilateral corticobulbar tract injury.

**Connect to Pattern Recognition:** The classic diplegic vignette (premature infant with PVL) maps to periventricular white matter injury. The dyskinetic variant maps to basal ganglia injury from hypoxia or bilirubin. The MRI findings align with the pathophysiological story.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
Most cases are multifactorial; a specific cause is found only in ~20%. Group into temporal categories with stem clues.

| Category | Example Causes | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Prenatal** (~50%) | Congenital brain malformation, intrauterine infection (TORCH, CMV), placental insufficiency, maternal trauma | Small head circumference, microcephaly, abnormal fetal movement, maternal infections |
| **Perinatal** (~30%) | Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy (HIE, e.g., placental abruption, cord prolapse, prolonged labour), neonatal stroke, kernicterus | Low Apgar scores, neonatal seizures, need for resuscitation, jaundice requiring exchange transfusion |
| **Postnatal** (~10%) | Meningitis/encephalitis, traumatic brain injury (non-accidental injury), hypoglycemic brain injury, cardiac arrest | Fever, meningitis, fall, inflicted head trauma; late onset after normal early development |
| **Genetic/metabolic** (~10%) | Rare: mutations in GNAO1, KANK1, etc.; metabolic disorders (arginase deficiency, glutaric aciduria) | Family history, atypical course (progressive? dyskinetic + dysmorphism), consanguinity |
| **Idiopathic** (~10–20%) | Unknown – often mild CP | No obvious risk factors; rule out progressive disorders |

**Most Common Cause Overall:** **Unknown** (idiopathic). However, in known cases, **perinatal HIE** in term infants and **PVL from prematurity** in preterm infants dominate.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria:**  
CP is a **clinical diagnosis** — no single test confirms it. Diagnosis relies on:
- Motor impairment (delayed milestones, abnormal tone, reflexes)
- Non-progressive course
- Exclusion of progressive disorders
- Supportive neuroimaging

**Key Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **MRI brain** | Structural injury: PVL, basal ganglia/thalamus lesions, stroke, malformations | Confirm non-progressive lesion; identify subtype; exclude mimics (tumour, leukodystrophy) | PVL (bilateral periventricular T2 hyperintensity) → spastic diplegia. Basal ganglia T1 hyperintensity → HIE/dyskinetic. Unilateral MCA territory → hemiplegic. **Normal MRI in ~15%** (especially mild CP). |
| **Cranial ultrasound** (neonatal) | Germinal matrix hemorrhage, PVL, ventriculomegaly | Bedside screening in preterm infants | Grade 3–4 hemorrhage or cystic PVL → high risk for CP. |
| **Developmental surveillance tools** (e.g., Hammersmith Infant Neurological Exam) | Neurological signs (tone, reflexes, motor milestones) | Early detection in high-risk infants (preterm, HIE) | Abnormal at 3–6 months → high probability of CP. |
| **Genetic testing** (array CGH, exome) | Copy number variants, mutations | If atypical features (dysmorphism, family history, normal MRI, progression) | Positive in ~10% of CP cases; can identify treatable metabolic causes (e.g., dopa-responsive dystonia). |
| **Metabolic workup** (ammonia, lactate, amino acids, organic acids) | Metabolic derangements | If episodic exacerbations, movement disorder, or regression | Abnormal → consider glutaric aciduria type 1, arginase deficiency, etc. (progressive mimics). |
| **EEG** | Epileptiform discharges | If seizures suspected (40% of CP) | Focal vs generalized; helps guide antiepileptic therapy. |

**Single Finding That Confirms / Rules Out:**  
- **Confirms CP:** clinical motor delay with a **consistent non-progressive picture** + appropriate MRI lesion (e.g., PVL). No single lab or imaging finding is pathognomonic, but **bilateral periventricular leukomalacia + spastic diplegia** is as close as it gets.  
- **Rules out CP:** **progressive loss of milestones**, normal serial MRIs over time, or a rapidly enlarging head (hydrocephalus, tumour). If a child loses skills (regression), think **inborn error of metabolism, leukodystrophy, or Rett syndrome**, not CP.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour — Newborn Period):**  
Only relevant for neonates at risk (e.g., HIE). The priority is neuroprotection.

- **Therapeutic hypothermia:** 33.5°C for 72 hours, started within 6 hours of birth (cooling caps for pillow? whole-body cooling) — reduces risk of CP in moderate-severe HIE (number needed to treat ~8 to prevent one CP).
- **Glucose, oxygenation, blood pressure optimisation:** Avoid hypoglycaemia (worsens injury), maintain normal ABGs, treat seizures.
- **Seizure control:** Phenobarbital 20 mg/kg IV load if >60 seconds or recurrent seizures — seizures worsen secondary energy failure.

**Why:** The window for intervention is narrow; hypothermia reduces apoptosis in basal ganglia and cerebral cortex.

**Ward (24–72 hours – Post-Stabilisation):**  
Focus on preventing complications and establishing baseline.

- **Tone management assessment:**
  - **Baclofen** (oral) 5 mg/kg/day divided q8h, titrate to effect (max 40 mg/day in children). GABA-B agonist reduces spasticity.
  - **Botulinum toxin A** (injections) – 5–10 U/kg per muscle (max 400 U total) for focal spasticity (e.g., gastrocnemius for toe-walking). Works by blocking acetylcholine at NMJ for 3–6 months.
- **Physical therapy (PT) and occupational therapy (OT):** Start stretching, passive range of motion, positioning (to prevent contractures), orthotics (ankle-foot orthoses for ankle plantarflexors).
- **Feeding assessment:** Speech therapy for oral motor dysfunction; consider nasogastric tube or gastrostomy if fails to thrive or aspiration risk (even in mild CP).
- **Parental education:** Explain “static encephalopathy” — the injury is done; avoid false hopes for cure but emphasize potential for improvement with therapy.

**Why:** Early intervention maximises neuroplasticity. Contractures develop rapidly; daily stretching necessary.

**Long-Term:**

- **Oral antispasticity:** **Baclofen** (as above), **tizanidine** (alpha-2 agonist – start 1 mg PO at night, titrate to 8 mg/day) or **dantrolene** (rarely used, risk of hepatotoxicity).
- **Intrathecal baclofen pump** (for severe generalised spasticity) – reduces spasticity without cognitive side effects.
- **Selective dorsal rhizotomy** (lumbar nerve root section) – best for spastic diplegia with good selective motor control. Can permanently reduce tone.
- **Orthopaedic surgery:** Tendon lengthening (e.g., gastrocnemius release, hamstring lengthening), osteotomies for hip subluxation.
- **Comorbidity management:**
  - Epilepsy → standard AEDs (levetiracetam 20–40 mg/kg/day, valproate 15–30 mg/kg/day).
  - Sialorrhea (drooling) → glycopyrrolate 0.04 mg/kg PO q8h, or botulinum toxin to salivary glands.
  - Neuropathic pain (muscle spasms, joint contractures) → gabapentin 5–10 mg/kg PO TID.
- **Multidisciplinary follow-up:** Neurology, physiatry, PT, OT, speech, ortho, social work, education support.

**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):**

- **Do not use diazepam chronically for spasticity** – tolerance, sedation, dependence; only for acute spasms (e.g., baclofen withdrawal).
- **Do not prescribe excessive doses of oral baclofen** — at >40 mg/day risk of sedation, confusion, and withdrawal seizures.
- **Do not delay surgery** — hip subluxation can progress to dislocation and chronic pain; screen with hip ultrasound annually in non-ambulatory children.
- **Do not label a child as CP if they lose skills (regression)** — always consider progressive disorders.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Cerebral Palsy | Leukodystrophy (e.g., Metachromatic) | Spinal Muscular Atrophy Type 2 | Rett Syndrome | Ataxic Telangiectasia |
|---------|----------------|--------------------------------------|-------------------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Pain** | Muscle spasticity, contracture | Usually no pain (neuropathy may cause dysesthesia) | None (floppy) | None | None |
| **Exam** | Spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski | Spasticity + optic atrophy + peripheral neuropathy | Hypotonia, areflexia, tongue fasciculations | Hand-wringing, dystonia, stereotypies | Ataxia, telangiectasias, immune deficiency |
| **Course** | **Non-progressive** (but may appear to worsen with growth) | **Progressive** (loss of milestones) | **Static** or gradual motor decline (non-UMN) | **Regressive** after 6–18 months normal development | **Progressive** ataxia after age 2 |
| **Imaging** | PVL, basal ganglia lesions, stroke, or normal | Diffuse white matter demyelination (tigroid, leuko) | Normal | Normal (or mild atrophy) | Cerebellar atrophy |
| **Key Diff** | No regression; onset <2 years; risk factors (prematurity, HIE). | Progressive; MRI shows demyelination; low arylsulfatase A (metachromatic). | Proximal muscle weakness; areflexia; normal intelligence; EMG shows denervation. | Deceleration of head growth; loss of speech; MECP2 mutation. | Ataxia with oculomotor apraxia; elevated AFP; immune defects. |

**Takeaway:** The single best differentiating feature for CP is **non-progression** — if milestones are consistently acquired (even if delayed), suspect CP. If a child stops gaining or loses skills, think of a progressive mimic.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major Complication 1: Hip Subluxation / Dislocation**  
- **How it presents:** Child with spastic quadriplegia or diplegia, asymmetrical leg length, decreased range of hip abduction, pain with diaper changes, loss of sitting balance.  
- **Key signs:** Asymmetric thigh folds, limited hip abduction (<30°).  
- **What to do:** Annual hip X-ray; if subluxation (migration percentage >30–40%), refer to orthopaedics for botulinum toxin or surgical reconstruction. Missed → chronic pain, osteonecrosis, scoliosis.

**Major Complication 2: Aspiration Pneumonia / Feeding Difficulties**  
- **How it presents:** Recurrent chest infections, wheezing, failure to thrive. Often subtle in mild CP.  
- **Key signs:** Wet cough, gurgly voice during feeds, oxygen desaturation after meals.  
- **What to do:** Videofluoroscopic swallow study; consider gastrostomy. If untreated → recurrent pneumonia, chronic lung disease, death.

**Major Complication 3: Epilepsy**  
- **How it presents:** 40% of CP patients develop seizures, often starting in first year. May be subtle (infantile spasms).  
- **Key signs:** Sterotyped events, hypsarrhythmia on EEG for infantile spasms.  
- **What to do:** EEG, start AED (e.g., levetiracetam). Untreated → cognitive decline, status epilepticus.

**Major Complication 4: Chronic Pain**  
- **How it presents:** Irritability, sleep disturbance, pain with passive movement (contractures, hip subluxation). Often underdiagnosed.  
- **Key signs:** Increased tone, joint deformities, children non-verbal may scream with handling.  
- **What to do:** Pain assessment (FLACC scale); treat spasticity; consider gabapentin, nerve blocks, NSAIDs.

**Late-Presentation Patterns (The Stems That Test “What Happens If Untreated”):**  
- **Adolescent with spastic diplegia:** Presents with severe hip dislocation, fixed flexion contractures, scoliosis, wheelchair-dependent despite good intelligence → missed early orthopaedic intervention.  
- **Adult with CP:** Presents with acute-on-chronic pain, pressure ulcers, malnutrition, osteoporosis → history of inadequate surveillance for hip dysplasia and feeding.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **CP TYPES (SDAHM)**  
   S – Spastic (most common)  
   D – Dyskinetic (athetoid)  
   A – Ataxic  
   H – Hypotonic (rare)  
   M – Mixed  

   *Memory trick: “Spastic Dyskinetic Ataxic Hypotonic Mixed” sounds like “SDAHM” = “Suddam” (Saddam Hussein’s effect on CP? Not polite, but memorable).*

2. **Risk Factors for CP: C.A.R. D.I.T.**  
   C – Congenital (TORCH, malformation)  
   A – Asphyxia (HIE)  
   R – Rh incompatibility (kernicterus)  
   D – Delivery complications (cord prolapse, placental abruption)  
   I – Infection (meningitis)  
   T – Trauma (non-accidental)  

   *Say: “Cars Dit” — your car’s check engine light? No, it’s for preventing CP.*

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**

1. **CP is static, but the clinical picture changes** — as a child grows, contractures worsen, epilepsy emerges, tremors become more obvious. This often leads parents (and doctors) to think it’s progressive. Always re-check history for missed regression.
2. **Premature infants with PVL → spastic diplegia; term infants with HIE → dyskinetic or quadriplegic CP.** This heuristic guides MRI expectations.
3. **Early hand preference before age 1 year is a red flag for hemiplegic CP** (normal children are ambidextrous until 18 months).
4. **The most common cause of death in CP is aspiration pneumonia** — aggressive feeding management prolongs life.
5. **Botulinum toxin is the first-line treatment for focal spasticity** but must be combined with stretching/orthotics. Over-treating without PT = no gain.
6. **Always screen for pain and hip dysplasia in non-ambulatory CP patients** — they cannot complain.
7. **If a child with CP develops acute worsening of symptoms, never assume it’s “just CP”** — rule out trauma, hydrocephalus, seizures, or infection.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**

- **Saying “It’s just CP” for new focal weakness or worse gait** — could be stroke, haemorrhage, or orthopaedic emergency.
- **Prescribing baclofen without a plan for withdrawal** — sudden stop can cause rebound spasticity, rhabdomyolysis, and seizures. Taper over 1–2 weeks.
- **Not referring for hip surveillance** — annual X-ray mandatory for GMFCS levels IV-V (non-walkers).
- **Ignoring feeding difficulties in mild CP** — children may compensate, but aspiration can be silent.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team:**

- **Status epilepticus:** Seizure >5 minutes or recurrent without recovery. Call crash team, administer benzodiazepine (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV or buccal midazolam 0.5 mg/kg). Follow PRISE protocol.
- **Aspiration pneumonia with respiratory failure:** Increased work of breathing, desaturation <92% despite O2, tachypnoea, copious secretions. Call anaesthetic/ICU for non-invasive ventilation or intubation.
- **Acute dystonic reaction (e.g., from metoclopramide or prochlorperazine):** Opisthotonos, oculogyric crisis, stridor. Call senior immediately; give diphenhydramine 1–2 mg/kg IV/IM (or procyclidine).
- **New-onset hip dislocation with acute pain:** Child screams with movement, cannot weight-bear. Obtain X-ray; orthopaedics consult within 24 hours; may require reduction under anaesthesia.
- **Suspected shunt dysfunction (if VP shunt for hydrocephalus):** Headache, vomiting, decreased GCS, sunset eyes. Call neurosurgery; CT head and shunt series urgent.

**Specific Thresholds (adapted for paediatrics – use PEWS or NEWS2 cautiously):**

| Parameter | Escalation Trigger | Action |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Respiratory rate** | >60 or <12 (infants), >40 or <10 (older child) | Call paediatric registrar or ICU |
| **Oxygen saturation** | <90% on high-flow O2 or >40% FiO2 | Arrange CXR, ABG, escalate |
| **Heart rate** | >180 (infant) or >150 (child) or <80 | ECG, fluid bolus, call senior |
| **GCS** | Drop by 2 points or total <8 | Urgent CT head, senior review, consider intubation |
| **NEWS2 (child, modified)** | Score ≥7 | Immediate medical registrar review; consider ICU |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L (in absence of seizure) | Sepsis workup, fluid resuscitation, repeat |

**Final Note:** CP is a lifelong diagnosis, but early intervention, multidisciplinary care, and aggressive management of comorbidities can dramatically improve quality of life. Always think twice before attributing new symptoms to the CP itself — it’s a static lesion

rawMarkdown:
# Cerebral Palsy

**One-liner:** A permanent, non-progressive motor disorder from fetal/infant brain injury; commonest childhood motor disability, requiring lifelong multidisciplinary care.

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 2-year-old boy presents with delayed walking. Parents note he “always seemed stiff” and started rolling over late. On exam, his legs are adducted and extended with scissoring, he walks on tiptoes (toe-walking), and ankle clonus is present. His arms are less affected, and he has a mild right-sided hand preference (but is left-handed). He sat at 12 months and is not yet walking. Birth history: born at 30 weeks, had respiratory distress syndrome, and required ventilation. MRI shows periventricular leukomalacia.

**Dissect the Vignette:**

- **Stiffness / scissoring / toe-walking** → Spastic diplegia, bilateral lower limb predominant — classic for periventricular white matter injury (prematurity)
- **Ankle clonus** → Upper motor neuron (UMN) sign — corticospinal tract damage
- **Right-sided hand preference at 2 years (left-handed)** → Asymmetric lesion; however, in spastic diplegia arms are often spared; if unilateral, suspect hemiplegic CP (stroke)
- **Delayed motor milestones (sitting at 12 months, not walking at 2 years)** → Motor delay, but note that CP is *non-progressive* — milestones should still advance, albeit slowly
- **Prematurity + RDS** → Major risk factor for periventricular leukomalacia (PVL) — ischemic damage to watershed areas in preterm brain
- **MRI periventricular leukomalacia** → Radiographic confirmation: bilateral cystic or gliotic changes around lateral ventricles

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Dyskinetic (athetoid) CP:** Child with fluctuating tone, writhing movements, delayed motor milestones, but spasticity is minimal. Often due to kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy) — ask about neonatal jaundice. Exam: choreoathetosis, rigidity, but reflexes may be normal.
- **Ataxic CP:** Presents with wide-based gait, intention tremor, delayed gross motor skills. No spasticity. Usually due to cerebellar injury (e.g., prenatal hemorrhage). Misdiagnosed as progressive cerebellar ataxia — key distinction: non-progressive course.
- **Hemiplegic CP (unilateral):** Child at 1–2 years with hand preference (early), arm held in flexed-adducted posture, leg circumducts in gait. Can mimic stroke; consider neonatal arterial ischemic stroke. MRI shows focal infarct.

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Cerebral palsy (CP) is a group of permanent disorders of movement and posture caused by non-progressive insults to the developing fetal or infant brain (before age ~2 years). The motor impairment is often accompanied by disturbances of sensation, cognition, communication, behaviour, and epilepsy. “Static encephalopathy” — the brain lesion does not worsen, but clinical manifestations may change with growth.

**Why This Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** CP affects 2–3 per 1000 live births — the most common motor disability in childhood.
- **Stakes:** It is a lifelong condition; many children have associated disabilities (epilepsy ~40%, intellectual disability ~50%, feeding difficulties ~30%) that drive morbidity more than the motor problem itself.
- **Common Pitfalls:** 
  - Mistaking CP for a progressive condition (e.g., leukodystrophy, metabolic disease) — leads to unnecessary tests or delayed diagnosis.
  - Attributing all symptoms to CP when new focal deficits appear — suspect stroke or infection.
  - Underestimating treatable comorbidities: spasticity can be managed with botulinum toxin, baclofen, or surgery; early intervention improves function.

## 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The Story of How CP Develops:**

The brain injury occurs during a critical window of development (fetal, perinatal, or early postnatal). Different timing and location produce different CP subtypes:

- **Preterm brain (24–32 weeks):** The periventricular white matter is vulnerable to ischemia and infection (chorioamnionitis). The watershed zones around the lateral ventricles are poorly perfused. This causes **periventricular leukomalacia (PVL)** — necrosis of oligodendrocytes → impaired myelination. Clinically → spastic diplegia (legs affected more than arms because descending corticospinal fibers to legs run closest to ventricles).
- **Term brain (36–40 weeks):** Acute hypoxia-ischemia (e.g., placental abruption, cord prolapse) damages the deep grey nuclei (basal ganglia, thalamus) due to high metabolic activity. This produces **dyskinetic CP** (choreoathetosis, rigidity). Alternatively, middle cerebral artery stroke (often unilateral) → **hemiplegic CP**.
- **Postnatal (<2 years):** Meningitis, traumatic brain injury, or encephalitis can cause diffuse injury → spastic quadriparesis or mixed CP.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

- **Spasticity** → UMN lesion: loss of inhibition of gamma motor neurons → hyperexcitable stretch reflex. Exam: velocity-dependent resistance to passive movement, clasp-knife phenomenon, clonus, Babinski sign.
- **Scissoring gait / adductor spasm** → Overactivity of hip adductors (UMN damage spares no thigh adductors but inhibits flexors).
- **Toe-walking** → Spastic gastrocnemius-soleus complex → plantarflexion at ankle.
- **Choreoathetosis** → Basal ganglia damage (putamen): loss of direct pathway inhibition → involuntary jerky (choreic) and writhing (athetoid) movements.
- **Ataxia** → Cerebellar damage: impaired coordination, intention tremor, wide-based gait.
- **Feeding difficulties / drooling** → Pseudobulbar palsy from bilateral corticobulbar tract injury.

**Connect to Pattern Recognition:** The classic diplegic vignette (premature infant with PVL) maps to periventricular white matter injury. The dyskinetic variant maps to basal ganglia injury from hypoxia or bilirubin. The MRI findings align with the pathophysiological story.

## 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

Most cases are multifactorial; a specific cause is found only in ~20%. Group into temporal categories with stem clues.

| Category | Example Causes | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Prenatal** (~50%) | Congenital brain malformation, intrauterine infection (TORCH, CMV), placental insufficiency, maternal trauma | Small head circumference, microcephaly, abnormal fetal movement, maternal infections |
| **Perinatal** (~30%) | Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy (HIE, e.g., placental abruption, cord prolapse, prolonged labour), neonatal stroke, kernicterus | Low Apgar scores, neonatal seizures, need for resuscitation, jaundice requiring exchange transfusion |
| **Postnatal** (~10%) | Meningitis/encephalitis, traumatic brain injury (non-accidental injury), hypoglycemic brain injury, cardiac arrest | Fever, meningitis, fall, inflicted head trauma; late onset after normal early development |
| **Genetic/metabolic** (~10%) | Rare: mutations in GNAO1, KANK1, etc.; metabolic disorders (arginase deficiency, glutaric aciduria) | Family history, atypical course (progressive? dyskinetic + dysmorphism), consanguinity |
| **Idiopathic** (~10–20%) | Unknown – often mild CP | No obvious risk factors; rule out progressive disorders |

**Most Common Cause Overall:** **Unknown** (idiopathic). However, in known cases, **perinatal HIE** in term infants and **PVL from prematurity** in preterm infants dominate.

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria:**  
CP is a **clinical diagnosis** — no single test confirms it. Diagnosis relies on:
- Motor impairment (delayed milestones, abnormal tone, reflexes)
- Non-progressive course
- Exclusion of progressive disorders
- Supportive neuroimaging

**Key Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **MRI brain** | Structural injury: PVL, basal ganglia/thalamus lesions, stroke, malformations | Confirm non-progressive lesion; identify subtype; exclude mimics (tumour, leukodystrophy) | PVL (bilateral periventricular T2 hyperintensity) → spastic diplegia. Basal ganglia T1 hyperintensity → HIE/dyskinetic. Unilateral MCA territory → hemiplegic. **Normal MRI in ~15%** (especially mild CP). |
| **Cranial ultrasound** (neonatal) | Germinal matrix hemorrhage, PVL, ventriculomegaly | Bedside screening in preterm infants | Grade 3–4 hemorrhage or cystic PVL → high risk for CP. |
| **Developmental surveillance tools** (e.g., Hammersmith Infant Neurological Exam) | Neurological signs (tone, reflexes, motor milestones) | Early detection in high-risk infants (preterm, HIE) | Abnormal at 3–6 months → high probability of CP. |
| **Genetic testing** (array CGH, exome) | Copy number variants, mutations | If atypical features (dysmorphism, family history, normal MRI, progression) | Positive in ~10% of CP cases; can identify treatable metabolic causes (e.g., dopa-responsive dystonia). |
| **Metabolic workup** (ammonia, lactate, amino acids, organic acids) | Metabolic derangements | If episodic exacerbations, movement disorder, or regression | Abnormal → consider glutaric aciduria type 1, arginase deficiency, etc. (progressive mimics). |
| **EEG** | Epileptiform discharges | If seizures suspected (40% of CP) | Focal vs generalized; helps guide antiepileptic therapy. |

**Single Finding That Confirms / Rules Out:**  
- **Confirms CP:** clinical motor delay with a **consistent non-progressive picture** + appropriate MRI lesion (e.g., PVL). No single lab or imaging finding is pathognomonic, but **bilateral periventricular leukomalacia + spastic diplegia** is as close as it gets.  
- **Rules out CP:** **progressive loss of milestones**, normal serial MRIs over time, or a rapidly enlarging head (hydrocephalus, tumour). If a child loses skills (regression), think **inborn error of metabolism, leukodystrophy, or Rett syndrome**, not CP.

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (First Hour — Newborn Period):**  
Only relevant for neonates at risk (e.g., HIE). The priority is neuroprotection.

- **Therapeutic hypothermia:** 33.5°C for 72 hours, started within 6 hours of birth (cooling caps for pillow? whole-body cooling) — reduces risk of CP in moderate-severe HIE (number needed to treat ~8 to prevent one CP).
- **Glucose, oxygenation, blood pressure optimisation:** Avoid hypoglycaemia (worsens injury), maintain normal ABGs, treat seizures.
- **Seizure control:** Phenobarbital 20 mg/kg IV load if >60 seconds or recurrent seizures — seizures worsen secondary energy failure.

**Why:** The window for intervention is narrow; hypothermia reduces apoptosis in basal ganglia and cerebral cortex.

**Ward (24–72 hours – Post-Stabilisation):**  
Focus on preventing complications and establishing baseline.

- **Tone management assessment:**
  - **Baclofen** (oral) 5 mg/kg/day divided q8h, titrate to effect (max 40 mg/day in children). GABA-B agonist reduces spasticity.
  - **Botulinum toxin A** (injections) – 5–10 U/kg per muscle (max 400 U total) for focal spasticity (e.g., gastrocnemius for toe-walking). Works by blocking acetylcholine at NMJ for 3–6 months.
- **Physical therapy (PT) and occupational therapy (OT):** Start stretching, passive range of motion, positioning (to prevent contractures), orthotics (ankle-foot orthoses for ankle plantarflexors).
- **Feeding assessment:** Speech therapy for oral motor dysfunction; consider nasogastric tube or gastrostomy if fails to thrive or aspiration risk (even in mild CP).
- **Parental education:** Explain “static encephalopathy” — the injury is done; avoid false hopes for cure but emphasize potential for improvement with therapy.

**Why:** Early intervention maximises neuroplasticity. Contractures develop rapidly; daily stretching necessary.

**Long-Term:**

- **Oral antispasticity:** **Baclofen** (as above), **tizanidine** (alpha-2 agonist – start 1 mg PO at night, titrate to 8 mg/day) or **dantrolene** (rarely used, risk of hepatotoxicity).
- **Intrathecal baclofen pump** (for severe generalised spasticity) – reduces spasticity without cognitive side effects.
- **Selective dorsal rhizotomy** (lumbar nerve root section) – best for spastic diplegia with good selective motor control. Can permanently reduce tone.
- **Orthopaedic surgery:** Tendon lengthening (e.g., gastrocnemius release, hamstring lengthening), osteotomies for hip subluxation.
- **Comorbidity management:**
  - Epilepsy → standard AEDs (levetiracetam 20–40 mg/kg/day, valproate 15–30 mg/kg/day).
  - Sialorrhea (drooling) → glycopyrrolate 0.04 mg/kg PO q8h, or botulinum toxin to salivary glands.
  - Neuropathic pain (muscle spasms, joint contractures) → gabapentin 5–10 mg/kg PO TID.
- **Multidisciplinary follow-up:** Neurology, physiatry, PT, OT, speech, ortho, social work, education support.

**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):**

- **Do not use diazepam chronically for spasticity** – tolerance, sedation, dependence; only for acute spasms (e.g., baclofen withdrawal).
- **Do not prescribe excessive doses of oral baclofen** — at >40 mg/day risk of sedation, confusion, and withdrawal seizures.
- **Do not delay surgery** — hip subluxation can progress to dislocation and chronic pain; screen with hip ultrasound annually in non-ambulatory children.
- **Do not label a child as CP if they lose skills (regression)** — always consider progressive disorders.

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Cerebral Palsy | Leukodystrophy (e.g., Metachromatic) | Spinal Muscular Atrophy Type 2 | Rett Syndrome | Ataxic Telangiectasia |
|---------|----------------|--------------------------------------|-------------------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Pain** | Muscle spasticity, contracture | Usually no pain (neuropathy may cause dysesthesia) | None (floppy) | None | None |
| **Exam** | Spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski | Spasticity + optic atrophy + peripheral neuropathy | Hypotonia, areflexia, tongue fasciculations | Hand-wringing, dystonia, stereotypies | Ataxia, telangiectasias, immune deficiency |
| **Course** | **Non-progressive** (but may appear to worsen with growth) | **Progressive** (loss of milestones) | **Static** or gradual motor decline (non-UMN) | **Regressive** after 6–18 months normal development | **Progressive** ataxia after age 2 |
| **Imaging** | PVL, basal ganglia lesions, stroke, or normal | Diffuse white matter demyelination (tigroid, leuko) | Normal | Normal (or mild atrophy) | Cerebellar atrophy |
| **Key Diff** | No regression; onset <2 years; risk factors (prematurity, HIE). | Progressive; MRI shows demyelination; low arylsulfatase A (metachromatic). | Proximal muscle weakness; areflexia; normal intelligence; EMG shows denervation. | Deceleration of head growth; loss of speech; MECP2 mutation. | Ataxia with oculomotor apraxia; elevated AFP; immune defects. |

**Takeaway:** The single best differentiating feature for CP is **non-progression** — if milestones are consistently acquired (even if delayed), suspect CP. If a child stops gaining or loses skills, think of a progressive mimic.

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major Complication 1: Hip Subluxation / Dislocation**  
- **How it presents:** Child with spastic quadriplegia or diplegia, asymmetrical leg length, decreased range of hip abduction, pain with diaper changes, loss of sitting balance.  
- **Key signs:** Asymmetric thigh folds, limited hip abduction (<30°).  
- **What to do:** Annual hip X-ray; if subluxation (migration percentage >30–40%), refer to orthopaedics for botulinum toxin or surgical reconstruction. Missed → chronic pain, osteonecrosis, scoliosis.

**Major Complication 2: Aspiration Pneumonia / Feeding Difficulties**  
- **How it presents:** Recurrent chest infections, wheezing, failure to thrive. Often subtle in mild CP.  
- **Key signs:** Wet cough, gurgly voice during feeds, oxygen desaturation after meals.  
- **What to do:** Videofluoroscopic swallow study; consider gastrostomy. If untreated → recurrent pneumonia, chronic lung disease, death.

**Major Complication 3: Epilepsy**  
- **How it presents:** 40% of CP patients develop seizures, often starting in first year. May be subtle (infantile spasms).  
- **Key signs:** Sterotyped events, hypsarrhythmia on EEG for infantile spasms.  
- **What to do:** EEG, start AED (e.g., levetiracetam). Untreated → cognitive decline, status epilepticus.

**Major Complication 4: Chronic Pain**  
- **How it presents:** Irritability, sleep disturbance, pain with passive movement (contractures, hip subluxation). Often underdiagnosed.  
- **Key signs:** Increased tone, joint deformities, children non-verbal may scream with handling.  
- **What to do:** Pain assessment (FLACC scale); treat spasticity; consider gabapentin, nerve blocks, NSAIDs.

**Late-Presentation Patterns (The Stems That Test “What Happens If Untreated”):**  
- **Adolescent with spastic diplegia:** Presents with severe hip dislocation, fixed flexion contractures, scoliosis, wheelchair-dependent despite good intelligence → missed early orthopaedic intervention.  
- **Adult with CP:** Presents with acute-on-chronic pain, pressure ulcers, malnutrition, osteoporosis → history of inadequate surveillance for hip dysplasia and feeding.

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **CP TYPES (SDAHM)**  
   S – Spastic (most common)  
   D – Dyskinetic (athetoid)  
   A – Ataxic  
   H – Hypotonic (rare)  
   M – Mixed  

   *Memory trick: “Spastic Dyskinetic Ataxic Hypotonic Mixed” sounds like “SDAHM” = “Suddam” (Saddam Hussein’s effect on CP? Not polite, but memorable).*

2. **Risk Factors for CP: C.A.R. D.I.T.**  
   C – Congenital (TORCH, malformation)  
   A – Asphyxia (HIE)  
   R – Rh incompatibility (kernicterus)  
   D – Delivery complications (cord prolapse, placental abruption)  
   I – Infection (meningitis)  
   T – Trauma (non-accidental)  

   *Say: “Cars Dit” — your car’s check engine light? No, it’s for preventing CP.*

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**

1. **CP is static, but the clinical picture changes** — as a child grows, contractures worsen, epilepsy emerges, tremors become more obvious. This often leads parents (and doctors) to think it’s progressive. Always re-check history for missed regression.
2. **Premature infants with PVL → spastic diplegia; term infants with HIE → dyskinetic or quadriplegic CP.** This heuristic guides MRI expectations.
3. **Early hand preference before age 1 year is a red flag for hemiplegic CP** (normal children are ambidextrous until 18 months).
4. **The most common cause of death in CP is aspiration pneumonia** — aggressive feeding management prolongs life.
5. **Botulinum toxin is the first-line treatment for focal spasticity** but must be combined with stretching/orthotics. Over-treating without PT = no gain.
6. **Always screen for pain and hip dysplasia in non-ambulatory CP patients** — they cannot complain.
7. **If a child with CP develops acute worsening of symptoms, never assume it’s “just CP”** — rule out trauma, hydrocephalus, seizures, or infection.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**

- **Saying “It’s just CP” for new focal weakness or worse gait** — could be stroke, haemorrhage, or orthopaedic emergency.
- **Prescribing baclofen without a plan for withdrawal** — sudden stop can cause rebound spasticity, rhabdomyolysis, and seizures. Taper over 1–2 weeks.
- **Not referring for hip surveillance** — annual X-ray mandatory for GMFCS levels IV-V (non-walkers).
- **Ignoring feeding difficulties in mild CP** — children may compensate, but aspiration can be silent.

## 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team:**

- **Status epilepticus:** Seizure >5 minutes or recurrent without recovery. Call crash team, administer benzodiazepine (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV or buccal midazolam 0.5 mg/kg). Follow PRISE protocol.
- **Aspiration pneumonia with respiratory failure:** Increased work of breathing, desaturation <92% despite O2, tachypnoea, copious secretions. Call anaesthetic/ICU for non-invasive ventilation or intubation.
- **Acute dystonic reaction (e.g., from metoclopramide or prochlorperazine):** Opisthotonos, oculogyric crisis, stridor. Call senior immediately; give diphenhydramine 1–2 mg/kg IV/IM (or procyclidine).
- **New-onset hip dislocation with acute pain:** Child screams with movement, cannot weight-bear. Obtain X-ray; orthopaedics consult within 24 hours; may require reduction under anaesthesia.
- **Suspected shunt dysfunction (if VP shunt for hydrocephalus):** Headache, vomiting, decreased GCS, sunset eyes. Call neurosurgery; CT head and shunt series urgent.

**Specific Thresholds (adapted for paediatrics – use PEWS or NEWS2 cautiously):**

| Parameter | Escalation Trigger | Action |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Respiratory rate** | >60 or <12 (infants), >40 or <10 (older child) | Call paediatric registrar or ICU |
| **Oxygen saturation** | <90% on high-flow O2 or >40% FiO2 | Arrange CXR, ABG, escalate |
| **Heart rate** | >180 (infant) or >150 (child) or <80 | ECG, fluid bolus, call senior |
| **GCS** | Drop by 2 points or total <8 | Urgent CT head, senior review, consider intubation |
| **NEWS2 (child, modified)** | Score ≥7 | Immediate medical registrar review; consider ICU |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L (in absence of seizure) | Sepsis workup, fluid resuscitation, repeat |

**Final Note:** CP is a lifelong diagnosis, but early intervention, multidisciplinary care, and aggressive management of comorbidities can dramatically improve quality of life. Always think twice before attributing new symptoms to the CP itself — it’s a static lesion
